title: What Siri commands does Snore Timeline support?
text: Snore Timeline offers four Siri actions. Start Recording: say "Start recording with Snore Timeline" or "Begin sleep recording in Snore Timeline." Stop Recording: say "Stop recording with Snore Timeline" or "End sleep recording in Snore Timeline." Episode Count: ask "How many snoring episodes in Snore Timeline" or "Get my episode count with Snore Timeline" to hear the number of episodes for a date (it defaults to yesterday). Open Date: say "Show today in Snore Timeline" or "Open Snore Timeline to today" to open the app to a specific day. These same actions are available as building blocks in the Shortcuts app.

title: Can I delay when recording actually starts after I tap Start?
text: Yes. The Recording Delay feature lets you wait before listening begins, which is handy if you want time to fall asleep first. Set the delay with the slider in Settings under the Recording section, anywhere from 0 to 180 minutes in 5-minute increments. While the delay counts down, the Live Activity shows a "Recording delayed" status with a timer for the minutes remaining, and recording begins automatically when the countdown reaches zero. When you start a session through Siri or a widget, you can include a delay of up to 120 minutes, and the app shows a notification confirming the delay.

title: What does the Live Activity show while I'm recording?
text: During an active session, the Live Activity on your iPhone lock screen shows the app name, a "Sleep Recording" label, a running timer of how long you've been recording, the number of detected episodes, the number of detected signals, and a red Stop button. As sounds are picked up, it shows the current detection with an icon and label, such as Snoring, Sleep Talking, Gasp, Cough, Loud Sound, Laughter, Screaming, or Crying. After about 8 seconds with no new sound, the status returns to "Listening" to keep the display uncluttered. If a recording delay is active, it shows a "Recording delayed" countdown instead.

title: Does Snore Timeline work with my Apple Watch?
text: Yes. While a session is recording, a Live Activity appears in your Apple Watch Smart Stack with a red REC indicator, a live timer, and either "Listening" or a count of detected signals. Snore Timeline also offers watch face widgets: a Sleep Score widget (circular, corner, and rectangular styles) that shows last night's score in a color gauge, and an inline widget that reads "Score [value] · [duration]" (for example "Score 84 · 6h 42m") or "Recording sleep" when a session is in progress.

title: Are there home screen widgets for Snore Timeline?
text: Yes. The Sleep Score widget comes in small and medium sizes for your iPhone home screen. It shows last night's sleep score along with Start and Stop recording controls and your episode count, so you can begin or end a session right from the home screen. The widget labels yesterday's data as "Last night" and, before noon, labels the current day's data as "Tonight" so the context matches the time of day.

title: Can the app wake me up in the morning?
text: Yes. Snore Timeline includes a Wake Up feature in Settings under the Sleep Schedule section. Turn it on, set a wake-up time (it defaults to 7:00 AM), and at that time the app stops your current recording. You can also switch on "Play alarm sound" to have an alarm go off at the same time, and choose the sound from Radar (the default), Classic, Pulse, Bright, or Alert. When the alarm sounds, you'll see an "Alarm Ringing" notification that says "Tap to dismiss." with a Stop button. Playing the alarm sound needs notifications enabled for Snore Timeline.

title: Why can't Siri or a widget start a recording?
text: A few things can stop a session from starting. If a recording is already running, you'll see "Recording is already in progress. Stop the current recording before starting a new one." If your device is low on storage, you'll see "Storage is full. Please free up space in Settings before recording." The app needs roughly 100 MB free to begin; you can free space by adjusting your storage limit or enabling auto-trim in Settings. If microphone access is off, you'll see a prompt to enable it under Settings, Privacy and Security, Microphone for Snore Timeline. Trying to stop when nothing is recording returns "No recording is currently active."

title: What's the difference between episodes and signals shown during recording?
text: On the Live Activity, the episode count (shown with a waveform icon) is the number of snoring episodes detected so far, while the signal count (shown with a moon icon) is the number of individual audio detections. These give you a quick sense of activity at a glance during the night. The full breakdown is available in the app afterward. Snore Timeline analyzes audio only and is meant for personal insight or for sharing with a doctor, not for diagnosing any condition.

title: Does any of my audio get uploaded to the cloud?
text: No. Snore Timeline records and analyzes everything directly on your device. None of your audio is sent to cloud servers or any external service. The sound analysis that detects snoring, sleep talking, and breathing sounds runs entirely on your iPhone using Apple's built-in on-device sound analysis. There is no account to create, and nothing leaves your phone unless you choose to export and share a file yourself.

title: Where exactly is my data stored and who can access it?
text: Your recordings and analysis are stored only in Snore Timeline's own private storage on your iPhone. Recording files are kept in a private area that does not show up in the Files app and cannot be opened by other apps. The only way to get a recording out of the app is to export it yourself. Nobody else can access this data unless they have your unlocked phone, and Snore Timeline never transmits it anywhere.

title: If I delete a recording, is it gone for good?
text: Yes. When you delete data in Snore Timeline, it is removed from your device permanently. You can delete a single night's data from Settings, which removes that night's audio, snoring signals, episodes, and sleep analysis. You can also permanently erase everything, including all recordings, episodes, sleep analysis, and exported files, using the hold-to-confirm delete-all button. Because the data lives only on your device and is not backed up to any Snore Timeline server, deletion is final.

title: Do you collect any analytics or tracking on me?
text: No. Snore Timeline does not collect analytics, usage tracking, or any personal data about you. There are no third-party trackers and no advertising. The app simply records and analyzes your sleep audio on your own device for your personal insight.

title: What permissions does Snore Timeline ask for and why?
text: Snore Timeline asks for microphone access so it can capture and analyze audio while you sleep. If you turn on Apple Health integration, it also asks for Health permissions to read your sleep data and write its own sleep analysis back. If you use Siri Shortcuts to start or stop recordings or check your sleep summary by voice, it asks for Siri access. You stay in control of each permission through iOS settings and the app's own settings, and the app works fully for recording and analyzing audio even if you grant only microphone access.

title: What sleep data does Snore Timeline read from and write to Apple Health?
text: If you enable Apple Health integration, Snore Timeline can read sleep data recorded by your Apple Watch, including sleep stages, heart rate, heart rate variability, oxygen saturation, respiratory rate, and wrist temperature (on supported devices). After a recording session it can write its own sleep analysis back to Apple Health, specifically sleep stages and respiratory rate measurements. You see a single Health permission screen listing every data type the app reads and writes, with a checkbox for each so you decide exactly what to allow.

title: How do I turn Apple Health integration on or off?
text: Apple Health integration is controlled by a single toggle in Snore Timeline's settings. Turning it off disables all reading from and writing to Apple Health at once. You can also fine-tune individual data types in the iOS Health app's privacy settings at any time. The app works fully for recording and analyzing audio even if you never connect it to Apple Health.

title: Can I delete only my audio files and keep my snore episodes and insights?
text: Yes. Snore Timeline offers a recordings-only deletion mode that removes your audio files while keeping the episodes and sleep analysis, so you preserve your insights and free up space. There is also an episode-only cleanup option that keeps your full audio recordings but removes the oldest episodes and analysis. These give you control over what to remove and what to keep.

title: Can Snore Timeline automatically manage storage so old recordings get deleted?
text: Yes. You can set a storage limit of 1, 2, 4, or 8 GB. When your data passes that limit, the app automatically deletes the oldest days to stay under it. You can choose whether auto-cleanup removes entire days of data or only the audio recordings (keeping your episodes and analysis), depending on what you want to keep. All of this happens on your device.

title: Does Snore Timeline send my data to my Apple Watch or other devices?
text: If you enable Apple Watch sync, Snore Timeline shares your recorded sleep data with the companion app on your own Apple Watch so you can view it there. This stays within Apple's own sync between your devices and is not sent to Snore Timeline servers. The app can also detect other health apps and wearables, such as Apple Watch, Oura, Whoop, and Garmin, that have written sleep data to Apple Health, so you can compare them, but it only reads what you have allowed in Apple Health.

title: Is the snore and breathing analysis a medical diagnosis?
text: No. Snore Timeline analyzes audio on your device to identify sounds like snoring, sleep talking, and breathing patterns. It is for personal insight and for sharing objective data with your doctor if you choose. It does not diagnose sleep apnea or any other medical condition, and it is not a medical device.

title: Why did my recording stop in the middle of the night?
text: The most common reason a recording stops overnight is another app taking over the microphone or audio. Snore Timeline automatically pauses when something else plays audio or grabs the mic, such as music, a podcast, a phone call, or a system alarm. When this happens you'll see 'Recording paused,' and the app automatically tries to resume on its own. It retries up to five times with increasing waits (1, 3, 7, 12, and 20 seconds). If it still can't get the microphone back after all five tries, it shows 'Can't resume recording' and stops. Recording can also stop if your device runs out of storage, in which case you'll see 'Storage full - recording stopped.' To reduce mid-night stops, close or quiet other audio apps before bed, make sure your phone has enough free space, and use Sleep Focus or Do Not Disturb so calls and alerts don't interrupt the session.

title: I woke up and the timeline is completely blank. What happened?
text: A blank timeline usually means recording never actually captured audio, or it stopped very early in the night. Check a few things. First, confirm microphone permission is granted under iOS Settings > Snore Timeline > Microphone; if it's off, the app can't record and shows 'Microphone unavailable. Please check permissions.' Second, check whether you had a Recording Delay set. During the delay window (which you can set up to 180 minutes) no audio is recorded, so if recording was interrupted or stopped during that period, nothing gets saved. Third, the app won't start if storage is too low; it shows 'Cannot record - only [X]MB free' in that case. Finally, if another app held the microphone the whole time, recording may have been blocked from the start. Look back at any status messages the app showed and verify your permissions and free space before the next session.

title: Did Low Power Mode or low battery stop my recording?
text: Snore Timeline records by holding the microphone active through the night, so the most reliable setup is to keep your phone plugged in while you sleep. If the battery runs down, the recording can end early. One simple way to cut overnight battery use is to place the phone face-down. iPhones with Always-On Display keep the screen lit even when locked, and leaving the phone face-up overnight can use about twice as much battery as placing it face-down, which turns the display off and roughly halves overnight drain. Keep the phone within one to two feet of you for the best detection while still being able to charge it. If you saw no recording at all rather than an early stop, also check microphone permission and available storage, since both can prevent recording from starting.

title: How do I keep notifications and calls from interrupting the recording overnight?
text: Snore Timeline pauses recording whenever another app or the system plays audio, which includes phone calls and some alerts. To avoid interruptions, turn on Sleep Focus or Do Not Disturb for your sleep hours so calls and notifications stay silent and don't grab the microphone. Keep other audio apps (music, podcasts, videos) fully closed before you start recording. If an interruption does happen, the app shows 'Recording paused' and automatically tries to resume up to five times, so a brief interruption usually recovers on its own. If you've set up the optional reminder to start recording, it's marked time-sensitive so it can still reach your lock screen during Sleep Focus; that reminder only prompts you to begin a session and does not interrupt one already in progress.

title: What do the recording status messages mean (paused, resumed, mic busy)?
text: Snore Timeline shows short status messages so you know what's happening. 'Recording paused' means another app or call took over audio and recording stopped temporarily. 'Attempting to resume recording...' and 'Retrying resume...' mean the app is trying to get the microphone back. 'Recording resumed!' confirms it succeeded. 'Can't resume recording' means it gave up after five tries. 'Mic busy - close other apps' means another app is holding the microphone, so close it and try again. 'Blocked by call or audio' means a phone call or system audio like an alarm is using the mic. 'Storage full - recording stopped' and 'Cannot record - only [X]MB free' both point to needing more free space on your device. 'Switched to [device name]' simply means your input changed (for example to headphones or a Bluetooth mic) and recording kept going without stopping.

title: Does Snore Timeline work with AirPods or other Bluetooth microphones?
text: Yes. If a Bluetooth microphone such as AirPods is connected, Snore Timeline detects it and uses it automatically, applying a small calibration adjustment so the volume readings stay consistent. If your input changes during the night, for example switching between the built-in mic, wired headphones, or a Bluetooth mic, the app shows 'Switched to [device name]' and recording continues without stopping. For overnight use, keep in mind that Bluetooth earbuds can run their own battery down and may disconnect when they do, which would change the input. For the most consistent all-night results, many people simply place the phone face-down one to two feet away and let the built-in microphone handle the recording.

title: Recording got interrupted and resumed. Will the recording delay start over?
text: No. The Recording Delay is a one-time 'time to fall asleep' timer that only applies at the start of a session. If recording is interrupted during the night and then resumes, the delay is not re-applied, so the app starts capturing audio again right away instead of going silent for another delay period. You can set the delay from 0 to 180 minutes in 5-minute increments under Recording Delay in Settings; during that initial window no audio is recorded, which gives you time to fall asleep without capturing the start of the night.

title: What should I do if the app says it can't record because storage is full?
text: Snore Timeline needs free space to save audio. If your device is low on space at the start it shows 'Cannot record - only [X]MB free' and won't begin; if space runs out mid-session it shows 'Storage full - recording stopped.' To prevent this, set a Storage Limit (1, 2, 4, or 8 GB) so the app automatically deletes audio from the oldest nights when the limit is reached. You can also enable Keep History so only audio files are removed while your episodes, sleep analysis, and timeline history are preserved, or turn on Episode-Only Storage to automatically strip silent portions and keep just the segments with detected episodes. Lowering the recording quality also shrinks file sizes. Freeing up general storage on your iPhone helps too.

title: How do I get the most reliable all-night recording?
text: A few habits make overnight recordings far more dependable. Keep the phone plugged in and place it face-down one to two feet away, which protects battery and turns off the Always-On Display while still capturing audio well. Turn on Sleep Focus or Do Not Disturb so calls and alerts don't pause recording, and close other audio apps before you start. Make sure microphone permission is granted in iOS Settings > Snore Timeline > Microphone, and confirm you have enough free space or have set a Storage Limit. Reduce constant background noise where you can, since fans, white noise machines, HVAC, air purifiers, open windows facing traffic, and TVs left playing can mask the sounds the app listens for.

title: What iPhone and iOS version do I need to use Snore Timeline?
text: Snore Timeline runs on iPhone and requires iOS 17.6 or later. The app is designed for portrait orientation and runs full screen. If your iPhone is on an older version of iOS, update it from Settings > General > Software Update before installing.

title: Does Snore Timeline work with Apple Watch?
text: Yes. Snore Timeline includes a companion Apple Watch app that lets you start and stop recordings from your wrist, so you do not have to reach for your iPhone after you are already in bed.

title: Can I start and stop recordings hands-free with Siri?
text: Yes. Snore Timeline supports Siri so you can start or stop a recording without touching your phone. Try saying "Hey Siri, start recording with Snore Timeline" or "Hey Siri, stop recording in Snore Timeline." This works from your lock screen, CarPlay, HomePod, or Apple Watch. You can also set a Bedtime Recording Reminder in Settings, which sends a time-sensitive notification at your chosen time so you can start tonight's recording with one tap, even during Sleep Focus.

title: Does recording all night drain my battery a lot?
text: Recording through the night does use battery since the microphone stays active while you sleep. On many iPhones the screen is a big factor. If your iPhone has an Always-On Display, leaving it face-up overnight can use about twice the battery of placing it face-down, because face-down automatically turns the display off. Snore Timeline suggests setting your phone face-down on the nightstand to cut overnight drain roughly in half. Many people also keep the phone plugged in while recording so it is fully charged in the morning.

title: Is it safe to charge my phone while it records all night?
text: Charging your iPhone while Snore Timeline records is a normal way to use the app, and it keeps your battery topped up through the whole night. Snore Timeline keeps recording in the background with the screen locked whether or not the phone is plugged in. For best detection, keep the phone about 1 to 2 feet from you, so use a cable that reaches your nightstand or bedside surface at that distance.

title: Can I record using my AirPods instead of the phone mic?
text: Yes. If AirPods or another Bluetooth microphone are connected when you start a recording, Snore Timeline uses that microphone automatically instead of the built-in iPhone mic. Keep in mind that AirPods sit in or near your ears, which changes how loud and clear sounds are captured compared to the phone mic. For the most consistent results, many people record with the iPhone placed 1 to 2 feet away, since that is the placement the detection is tuned around.

title: How does the app handle different time zones when I travel?
text: Snore Timeline uses your iPhone's current local time zone to label and organize recordings. When you travel and your phone updates to a new time zone, new recordings appear in that local time, so a night recorded in a new location shows at the local clock time you went to bed there.

title: Can I make the text bigger in Snore Timeline?
text: Yes. Snore Timeline supports Dynamic Type, so the app's text follows the size you set on your iPhone. To change it, go to Settings > Accessibility > Display & Text Size > Larger Text and adjust the slider. The app's text scales to match your choice.

title: Does Snore Timeline work with VoiceOver and other accessibility features?
text: Snore Timeline includes accessibility labels on its interface elements to support VoiceOver and other assistive technologies, alongside Dynamic Type for adjustable text sizes. This helps make navigating the timeline and settings more accessible.

title: What languages is Snore Timeline available in?
text: Snore Timeline is localized into English, German, Spanish, French, Turkish, and Simplified Chinese. The app follows your iPhone's language setting and shows a supported language automatically when one matches.

title: Can Snore Timeline sync with Apple Health?
text: Yes. Snore Timeline can integrate with Apple Health. It can read your sleep data to show insights and trends, and after a recording it can write sleep analysis, sleep stages (light, deep, REM, awake), and respiratory rate during sleep back to Apple Health. You enable this in Settings under Apple Health. These figures are for personal insight, not a medical diagnosis.

title: How much free storage do I need to record with Snore Timeline?
text: You need at least 100 MB of free space to open the app, and about 500 MB free to start a new recording. If your device storage gets critically low, Snore Timeline falls back to in-memory operation to help prevent data loss. To keep storage use down over time, you can turn on Episode-Only Storage in Settings, which automatically removes silent stretches after each session while keeping all of your episode data, and you can choose a lower recording quality (low, standard, or high) in Settings.
